title: Instruction-Following Evaluation for Large Language Models
authors: Jeffrey Zhou, Tianjian Lu, Swaroop Mishra, Siddhartha Brahma, Sujoy Basu, Yi Luan, Denny Zhou, Le Hou (Google; Jeffrey Zhou affiliated with Yale University, work done at Google)
venue: arXiv preprint, cs.CL (cross-listed cs.AI, cs.LG); v1 14 November 2023; not published at a peer-reviewed venue as of the arXiv listing consulted
claim: The paper introduces IFEval, a benchmark for evaluating LLM instruction-following that focuses on a category of 'verifiable instructions' (e.g., 'write more than 400 words', 'mention the keyword AI at least 3 times') whose compliance can be checked by simple, deterministic, interpretable programs rather than human or model-based judges. It defines 25 types of verifiable instructions, constructs 541 prompts (each containing one or more such instructions), and reports strict and loose prompt-level and instruction-level accuracy for GPT-4 and PaLM 2 Small as baseline results.
novelty: The authors present the contribution as: (1) identifying and formalizing a category of 'verifiable instructions' that admit fully automatic, objective, reproducible compliance checking, in contrast to human evaluation (expensive, slow, subjective) and model-based/LLM-judge evaluation (potentially biased, limited by evaluator quality); (2) a concrete taxonomy of 25 verifiable instruction types across 9 groups (Keywords, Language, Length Constraints, Detectable Content, Detectable Format, Combination, Change Cases, Start with/End with, Punctuation) with example phrasings and multiple parameter/phrasing variants; (3) a 4-step synthetic-plus-curated prompt synthesis pipeline (base prompt + random instruction sampling, few-shot filtering of illogical prompts, few-shot rephrasing for diversity, manual edit pass) yielding 541 prompts; (4) a dual strict/loose scoring scheme (Eq. 1 is_followed and Eq. 2 is_followed_loose with 8 response transformations) to reduce false negatives from formatting artifacts like markdown bolding or intro/outro lines, while acknowledging this trades off toward more false positives; and (5) released code/data (GitHub link in abstract) enabling reproducibility. Section 4 explicitly frames current limitations (diversity/quantity of instructions, no multi-modal support) as future work, i.e. IFEval v1 is presented as a first, deliberately simple/extensible instantiation rather than a final or exhaustive benchmark.

protocol.description: Each evaluation instance is a single natural-language prompt containing a base request plus one to three (occasionally more, via later composition) appended verifiable instructions in one call; the model produces one response, which is then run through the deterministic checker for each instruction embedded in that prompt. There is no multi-turn interaction, no feedback loop, and no per-turn partial credit shown to the model — the model sees only the prompt text once. Section 2.1: 'we generate a set of base prompts with one to three randomly selected verifiable instructions appended to the end of each prompt.' The paper does not describe any interactive/iterative protocol, chain-of-thought scaffolding, few-shot demonstrations given to the *evaluated* model, or algorithm-in-prompt guidance for the evaluated model; few-shot prompting is used only internally by the authors to generate/filter/rephrase prompts (Section 2.1), not as an in-context strategy shown to the evaluated GPT-4/PaLM 2 models. Table 2 shows the exact prompt format the evaluated models receive, e.g.: 'Who built the first artificial ice rink? Please include the keys (1) Name (2) Location and (3) Year. Use less than 487 words.'
protocol.observation_content: The evaluated model sees only the full natural-language prompt (base task text concatenated with the appended verifiable-instruction phrasing) in a single forward pass/API call; it does not see the checker code, the instruction taxonomy, worked examples of compliant responses, or any scoring feedback before answering. No demos or in-context exemplars are shown to the evaluated model in the reported setup (Section 2.1, Table 2, Section 3: 'We scrapped GPT-4 and PaLM 2 S responses ... through API calls').

representations.variants_compared: unverified — missing from extraction: the paper does not report an ablation across alternative input/prompt representations (e.g., JSON-structured constraint lists vs. natural-language sentences, or different phrasing templates) with corresponding accuracy deltas. It does state (Section 1, Section 2.1) that each verifiable instruction has multiple parameter variants (e.g., 'write 450 to 500 words' vs. 'write 350 to 400 words') and multiple phrasing variants (e.g., 'write 450 to 500 words' vs. 'your response must contain 450 to 500 words'), used to increase prompt diversity via few-shot rephrasing, but no accuracy breakdown by phrasing variant vs. parameter variant is given in the extracted text.
representations.note: Figure 2 and Figure 3 (both image-only in this extraction, captions preserved) break down instruction-level strict-accuracy by instruction category/detailed category — this is the closest the paper gets to a 'representation' comparison (accuracy varies by *instruction type*, not by prompt-format variant), but the actual per-category numbers are unverified — missing from extraction (figure content not renderable as text per the header's noted gap).

models.name: GPT-4
models.strategies: Zero-shot single-call prompting only; no CoT, few-shot, many-shot, ReAct, or algorithm-in-prompt strategies applied to the evaluated model (Section 3: responses 'scrapped ... through API calls' in November 2023).
models.key_results: Table 3: Prompt-level strict-accuracy 76.89%, Inst-level strict-accuracy 83.57%, Prompt-level loose-accuracy 79.30%, Inst-level loose-accuracy 85.37%.

models.name: PaLM 2 S (Small)
models.strategies: Zero-shot single-call prompting only; responses scrapped via API calls in August 2023 (Section 3).
models.key_results: Table 3: Prompt-level strict-accuracy 43.07%, Inst-level strict-accuracy 55.76%, Prompt-level loose-accuracy 46.95%, Inst-level loose-accuracy 59.11%.

headline_numbers: 25 verifiable instruction types across 9 groups, and 541 prompts total (Abstract; Section 1; Table 1).
GPT-4: 76.89% prompt-level strict-accuracy, 83.57% inst-level strict-accuracy, 79.30% prompt-level loose-accuracy, 85.37% inst-level loose-accuracy (Table 3).
PaLM 2 S: 43.07% prompt-level strict-accuracy, 55.76% inst-level strict-accuracy, 46.95% prompt-level loose-accuracy, 59.11% inst-level loose-accuracy (Table 3).

Prompt-level strict-accuracy requires ALL verifiable instructions in a prompt to be followed (Section 3, metric definition 1) — i.e., all-or-nothing scoring at the prompt level, analogous to c22's all-pass 0/1 criterion.

Loose accuracy is computed by taking Any(...) over 8 response transformations (identity, markdown-strip, first-line-strip, last-line-strip, and their pairwise/triple combinations) per Eq. 2, explicitly to reduce false negatives at the cost of introducing false positives (Section 2.2).
Note the paper is internally inconsistent on prompt count: abstract says 'around 500 prompts,' Section 1 and the header's extraction note both confirm the precise figure is 541 prompts.
failure_modes: The paper's own discussion of limitations (Section 4) is at the benchmark-design level, not a per-instruction-type model failure analysis in the extracted prose: it states the current 25-instruction taxonomy should have its 'diversity and quantity' increased, and that the benchmark should be 'extend[ed] to multi-modal use cases' (e.g., 'generate at least 3 images of...'). At the metric level, Section 2.2 explicitly documents a failure mode of the strict checker itself: false negatives arise when a model correctly follows an instruction (e.g., ending with an exact phrase) but the response contains incidental markdown formatting (e.g., bold '**' around the required end phrase) or extraneous intro/outro lines, causing naive string matching to misclassify compliance as failure. The loose metric is introduced specifically to counter this, at the acknowledged cost of possible false positives (e.g., a word-count instruction spuriously judged 'followed' after the first line, which might contain most of the disqualifying word count, is stripped). Model-level per-category failure numbers are presented only in Figure 2 (instruction-level strict-accuracy by instruction category) and Figure 3 (per detailed category, Appendix Section 5), both of which are image-only in this extraction (unverified — missing from extraction, per the header's noted figure gap).
one_shot_vs_multistep: The paper only evaluates and only claims results for single forward-pass, single-call instruction following: 'We scrapped GPT-4 and PaLM 2 S responses in November and August of 2023, respectively, through API calls' (Section 3), with no interaction loop, no multi-turn refinement, no critique-and-revise step, and no demonstration/few-shot conditioning applied to the evaluated model itself. All reported accuracy numbers (Table 3) are for this one-shot regime. The paper draws no conclusions about what models can do across an interaction loop, with iterative feedback, or with more in-context demonstrations — that comparison is simply outside its scope; the few-shot prompting mentioned in Section 2.1 is used by the authors only to construct/filter/rephrase the benchmark's prompts, not as a strategy given to (or studied for) the models being evaluated.

relation_to_candidate.position: IFEval sits squarely at the single-shot end of the family's protocol axis: one prompt in, one response out, checked by a deterministic program (is_followed / is_followed_loose), with no multi-turn or interactive component anywhere in the paper. What is observed by the model is exactly the composed natural-language prompt (base request + 1-3 appended verifiable instructions in one string, per Table 2 and Section 2.1) — there is no separate demonstration phase, no partial-credit signal fed back mid-generation, and no per-checker diagnostic surfaced to the model (diagnostics are computed only post-hoc by the authors, per instruction category, for reporting in Figure 2/3). Output shape is a free-form natural-language response (an email, essay, poem, JSON blob, etc.) of the model's own choosing in length/structure — not a single fact/label — evaluated by scanning that full response against each embedded instruction's checker function. The verifier type is a deterministic, interpretable Python-style checking function per instruction type (25 distinct checker functions, Table 1), run either in 'strict' mode (raw response) or 'loose' mode (best of 8 mechanically transformed variants of the response, Eq. 2) — never an LLM-judge or human rater.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: IFEval is the direct ancestor and checker-library source for c22: c22's own file states it 'reuses [IFEval's] deterministic checker library (25 instruction types) as the constraint-atom source' and its constraint categories in the illustrative example (exact word count, forbidden letter/keyword, casing, end-with token) map one-to-one onto IFEval's Table 1 groups (Length Constraints/Number Words, Keywords/Forbidden Words and Include Keywords, Change Cases/All Lowercase or All Uppercase, Start with/End with/End Checker). Both share the core scoring philosophy: a battery of independent, non-tautological, deterministic per-constraint checks; IFEval's prompt-level strict-accuracy ('all verifiable instructions in each prompt are followed', Section 3, metric 1) is exactly c22's 'scores 1 iff ALL checkers pass (0/1, no partial credit).' Both are single-call/single-shot at the point of generation. Both compose multiple (IFEval: 1-3 per prompt, per Section 2.1; c22: 3-5) atomic verifiable instructions into one prompt.
relation_to_candidate.differences: (1) Composition structure: IFEval's per-prompt instructions are sampled independently and simply concatenated/rephrased into fluent prose (Section 2.1) with no logical relationship enforced between them beyond avoiding 'obvious conflicts' — it has no conditional/Selection constraint type ('if property P then rule A else rule B') anywhere in Table 1's 25 instruction types; this conditional-discovery mechanism is c22's addition, sourced instead from ComplexBench per c22's own lineage notes. (2) Task substance: IFEval's base prompts are substantive open-ended requests (write an essay, a poem, a resume, an itinerary — see Appendix Section 6 example prompts), producing typically paragraph-length free-form output, whereas c22 deliberately trivializes the base task ('name a color') so nearly all of the task's difficulty and signal comes from the constraint stack rather than the underlying content generation. (3) Scale/generation process: IFEval is a fixed, frozen, human-curated-and-filtered set of 541 prompts built via few-shot generation + manual editing (Section 2.1) — a static released dataset, not a programmatic infinite generator; c22 explicitly wants a synthetic, seeded, infinite/contamination-resistant instance generator (a COLLIE-style grammar) that IFEval itself does not provide (this generator layer is exactly what c22's implementation review flags as net-new, unshipped work). (4) Diagnostics: IFEval computes per-instruction-category breakdowns only as an aggregate post-hoc research artifact (Figure 2/3, across many prompts) for benchmark reporting, not as a per-instance, per-checker diagnostic fact logged for each individual example the way c22 proposes. (5) Metric leniency: IFEval defines both a strict and an explicitly more lenient loose-accuracy variant (Eq. 2, 8 transformations) precisely because naive strict checking produces false negatives on real model output; c22's design as described commits only to strict 0/1 all-pass scoring with no loose/robustness layer mentioned, meaning it inherits IFEval's checker code but not IFEval's own mitigation for the false-negative problem that motivated loose-accuracy in the first place.
relation_to_candidate.what_c22_would_add: Beyond IFEval, c22 would add: (a) a programmatic, seeded, reproducible instance generator that samples base tasks and constraint subsets on demand (IFEval has no generator — it is a frozen 541-prompt release); (b) conditional Selection-type constraints requiring the model to discover a hidden if/else rule from properties of its own answer or the input, a constraint *type* absent from IFEval's 25-item taxonomy; (c) deliberate base-task triviality so that the compositional constraint-satisfaction problem, not open-ended content quality, is the entire measured skill; (d) an explicit incremental difficulty dial (constraint count/level, and OOD atom families per IFBench) as a controlled independent variable, which IFEval does not manipulate (IFEval's 1-3 count is a fixed sampling range, not a studied difficulty axis); and (e) per-instance, per-checker diagnostic logging as a first-class output for every generated example, rather than only aggregate category-level accuracy reporting after the fact.

corpus_claim: IFEval (Zhou et al., Nov 2023) is the canonical verifiable-instruction benchmark; c22 reuses its deterministic checker library (25 instruction types) as the constraint-atom source.
corpus_claim_verdict: supported. The paper text directly confirms both halves of the claim: (1) it defines exactly '25 types of those verifiable instructions' (Abstract; Section 1: 'Altogether, we create a list of 25 verifiable instructions'; enumerated in full in Table 1), each paired with a description amenable to deterministic/programmatic checking (Section 1: 'a simple, interpretable, and deterministic program to verify if corresponding responses follow the instructions or not'); and (2) the paper explicitly frames itself as introducing this checker-based evaluation approach for the first time relative to prior human- and model-based evaluation methods (Section 1's three-category critique of existing approaches), consistent with 'canonical' status as the originating verifiable-instruction benchmark that later work (IFBench, ComplexBench, FollowBench, etc., per c22's own lineage list) builds on. Minor precision note: the benchmark's own prompt count is 541, not 500 as the abstract's rounded phrase ('around 500 prompts') might suggest if quoted loosely — c22's claim does not cite a prompt count, so this does not affect the claim's verdict, but should be corrected to 541 if prompt count is ever cited from this paper.
baseline_relevant: Fixed dataset size: 541 prompts total (Section 1), each with 1 or more (sampled 1-3 in construction) verifiable instructions appended (Section 2.1) — useful as a scale reference for how many composed constraints per instance prior work found tractable/diverse enough to hand-curate.

Difficulty dial used by IFEval: number of instructions per prompt (1 to 3, randomly sampled at construction, Section 2.1) — a coarser, less controlled version of c22's proposed 3-5 constraint-count dial; IFEval does not report accuracy stratified by this count in the extracted text (unverified — missing from extraction / not present in main text or captured tables).

Metric floors/ceilings observed: GPT-4 prompt-level strict-accuracy 76.89% is far from ceiling even for a frontier model on relatively modest 1-3-constraint composition (Table 3); PaLM 2 S prompt-level strict-accuracy 43.07% shows a much lower floor for a smaller model, suggesting that strict all-pass scoring over multiple stacked constraints can substantially compress accuracy for weaker models — directly relevant to c22's own noted risk that 'strict all-pass over many atoms can floor a small model near zero.'

Two accuracy granularities used: prompt-level (all instructions in a prompt must pass) and instruction-level (fraction of individual instructions, pooled across prompts, that pass) — Table 3, Section 3 metrics 1-4. c22's proposed scoring (score 1 iff all checkers pass) corresponds exactly to IFEval's prompt-level strict-accuracy; the paper's own inst-level numbers (which are always higher than prompt-level, e.g. GPT-4 83.57% vs 76.89%) empirically demonstrate the signal-compression effect of all-pass scoring versus per-atom partial credit, directly relevant to designing baseline difficulty strata.

Strict vs. loose metric gap: loose accuracy is consistently a few points higher than strict for both models (GPT-4: +2.41pp prompt-level, +1.80pp inst-level; PaLM 2 S: +3.88pp prompt-level, +3.35pp inst-level; Table 3), quantifying how much of the 'failure' signal under naive strict checking is actually formatting-artifact false negatives rather than true non-compliance — relevant if c22's baseline checker needs a similar leniency layer to avoid penalizing trivial formatting choices.

No contamination controls or data-release-date caveats are discussed for the prompts themselves beyond release on GitHub (Abstract: code and data released at https://github.com/google-research/google-research/tree/master/instruction_following_eval) — the dataset is fully public/static, meaning direct reuse of IFEval's literal prompts (not just its checker code) risks contamination for any newly-trained/evaluated model; c22 avoids this by generating fresh instances synthetically rather than reusing IFEval's fixed 541 prompts.

Prompt synthesis pipeline documented in Section 2.1 (random instruction sampling -> few-shot illogical-prompt filtering -> few-shot rephrasing for diversity -> manual edit) is a concrete precedent for how to avoid 'boilerplate' one-shot-guessable phrasing when composing multiple constraints into a single natural prompt, relevant to c22's own composition/serialization step.
quotes: "We identified 25 types of those verifiable instructions and constructed around 500 prompts, with each prompt containing one or more verifiable instructions." (Abstract)
"Altogether, we create a list of 25 verifiable instructions. We further create a set of 541 prompts, with each prompt containing one or multiple verifiable instructions." (Section 1)

"These verifiable instructions are atomic instructions for which one can use a simple, interpretable, and deterministic program to verify if corresponding responses follow the instructions or not." (Section 1, summary)

"is_followed(resp,inst) = {True, if instruction is followed. False, otherwise.} ... We use Equation 1 to compute the instruction following accuracy, and refer to it as the strict metric." (Section 2.2, Eq. 1)
"is_followed_loose(resp,inst) = Any(is_followed(transform_t(resp), inst) for t=1,2,...) ... there are in total of eight transformations." (Section 2.2, Eq. 2)

"1. Prompt-level strict-accuracy: The percentage of prompts that all verifiable instructions in each prompt are followed. 2. Inst-level strict-accuracy: The percentage of verifiable instructions that are followed." (Section 3)
GPT-4: 76.89 / 83.57 / 79.30 / 85.37 (Prompt-level strict / Inst-level strict / Prompt-level loose / Inst-level loose accuracy, %); PaLM 2 S: 43.07 / 55.76 / 46.95 / 59.11 (Table 3)
"1. Increase the diversity and quantity of verifiable instructions. 2. Extend to multi-modal use cases. For example: 'generate at least 3 images of ...'." (Section 4, Discussion and future work)
